author: @BurntKittyForge-pl8ol
text: i have space but i demolitioned down one old shed built like a real room , guest room by itself and the wood rot was bad, end of lifespan, but the fiberglass had like a cockroach b*mb went off , from the moisture and wood , i didn't want to build that way seeing the final death stage , i use acrylics (cement opposed to portland hardeners but those are good too , just different ) , rebar masonry , CMU's ...some things are post beam then metal roofing in 3 foot widths with special washer screws (i like wood-x) ...easy my problem is i wanted to use masonry version of post beams design and it takes a while to engineer ...this type space requires either a lot of money OR alternative /recycled materials , one vid for example styrofoam mixed with concrete powder dust had flammability resistance better than other constructuion grade closed cell foam, My point on this particular material , i am designing a styrofoam shredder from old circular sawblades on threaded rod between bushings hopefully inside two 5 gallon buckets that revolve for the "door" opening to stuff foam , then i have about 5 years worth of 1 gallon milk jugs at about 2x week stack i want to fill with the shredded foam , then cover with either chicken wire, scrap wire wraps or hopefully boen type 3 foot x 150 foot fiberglass screen and then acrylic fake blocks or stones for wall nonsense, like amusement park stuff or putt-putt golf builds for wall sheds , durable well insulated, sound proofing design ...for example, i like log mills for under $3000 ideas and 3D PRINTER (MAYBE ????!?! THANKYOU < YES 3D .../lol...welding machines etc.... sidestreams of income, i scrap metal in past ...i like those sand p[lastic bricks seems easy for big backyard maybe I'd do a bunch hot into big multi press and run them over for the "press " By the way more later , the name is unfortgettable to search for me Sir, God Bless.. back tovid now...later...

author: @BurntKittyForge-pl8ol
text: very cool coincedence, I'm at a point working on property , i have shed space(s) but even those need rebuild , it's a tricky demo/engineer /reconstruct simultaneously mostly for signature ...been nonstop trying to sort out viable from sci -fi from the crawl to walk steps anyway , some still included but future and it is possible to forget dreams when you have so many /lol ranting i need investment tools, sales, industry just to counter the dollar devalue is the thing ,just to make the value itself back is a need to hussle...

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text:
This mindset is exactly why I have been powering forward with developing 3D printer upgrades to bring them to the level of "replicator" both for manufacturing things from stuff and for taking things and turning things into stuff (not just built but natural raw materials) like being able to hyper quickly oxidizing say iron bearing material into various oxides including rust (iron oxide) that I have already shown how easily rust can be turned back into raw iron deposited metal on a surface with a 405nm 500mW diode laser, (also broke down TiO2 white powder with it reforming into black and dark brown TiO2 and Ti2O3 I believe the other stuff is, all in open air with no other function. next will be to build an attraction system that super tight spaces of this being done will separate the oxygen from it using both the gravitational potential differences and electrostatic charges so depositing even hard to separate oxide metals will be able to be deposited as pure metal, :) 

Many thanks to you for showing how light (strobes at the time) can be used to de-oxidize metal by way of your video showing the copper oxide nano particles being fused into solid  metal via that high powered lighting. Just took thinking about the effect, knowing a bit about frequency of photons and how it effects their eV potentials to embrace trying higher UV range lighting specifically for this, as much  as possible of course as we still need UV in much higher frequencies that are also as efficient as diode tech :) which I have some potential directions to go for that too  :) (lots of these ideas often come from research on oil industry technologies like how to make mesopore alumina and zeolites ect vs the nano pore aluminum oxide anodized surfaces via acid and electrolysis :)

author: @unicornadrian1358
text: Another amazing video Rob! It's kind of funny seeing people complain that you used to be hand tool and recycled scrap channel. When I started following you, you were building batteries and experimenting with different carbon powders from a variety of materials. I for one love the flow and creativity in your videos and truly appreciate the journey you have brought us along. A saying I live by is, "If the only tool in your toolbox is a hammer, all your problems begin to look like nails." I am a die hard hand tool fan, but following along with you has encouraged me to broaden my horizons and I now have a CNC laser in my tool box! Thank you for everything you do Rob.

author: @richardglickman7203
text:
Though your channel is not so much about tools it has morphed. 
Moving from your workshop to your garage and now your house has impacted your content. It’s been a while since you’ve experimented with battery technologies, generators/alternators, human powered food blenders, paper bicycles, or triboelectricity.
Each of the above experiments could have been the beginning of an unbroken path to the recent sun/earth/moon orbit simulator.
3D and laser printers indeed make experimentation easier and faster but I doubt either will set you on a path to triboelectricity or paper bicycles.
I hope I’m wrong. The s/e/m simulator was entertaining. But the above experiments were all fun and stimulating.

Additive manufacting is certainly here to stay!  Cheers Rob!

author: @grendel1960a
text: I enjoy the luxury of both worlds, I have 3d printers, I have lathes and milling mchines, and while the lathes and mills arent the biggest out there, they are big enough, and I enjoy using them. I also enjoy CAD and creating the designs for 3d parts, sometimes I will combine the two. recently I picked up a seadoo underwater propulsion unit at a car boot sale- it was without the battery (available for £600) but I knew I could buy a standard battery and make supports for it for £70 . the supports took me 10 minutes design in CAD and about 2 hours printing (I am experimenting using the gyroid supports and a lower infill, and that is working out with stiffer parts) I do need to make some lugs to fit the battery connections, but I am thinking that will be an hours lathe work with some brass to make a post I can bolt the battery cables to, so mixed techniques. to be honest 3d printing is usually quicker even for one off parts, but when it needs to be metal to take the current flow or heat, then the lathe trumps out. You could say my home has changed, it is not only my work space for my job, but it is also evolving into my personal workshop. (and just for clarity I 3d scanned the part I needed to fit the battery into and then brought that into CAD to generate the supports i needed for the battery) modern technology is wonderful.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
